params.text:
The communication cycle

Communication works as a cycle. A sender has a message they want to share. They choose how to send it — spoken words, writing, a gesture or a picture. The receiver takes in that message and makes sense of it. They then give feedback, such as a nod, a reply or a puzzled look, which tells the sender whether the message got through.

Feedback is what turns one-way talking into real communication. It lets you check understanding and adjust as you go. If a person looks confused, you can slow down, rephrase, or try a different method rather than carrying on regardless. Without feedback you are only guessing whether you have been understood.

How it breaks down

At any point the cycle can fail. The sender may be unclear or use jargon. The message may be lost in a noisy room or a rushed handover. The receiver may mishear, be distracted, or understand the words differently from how they were meant. Even a small gap at one stage can change the whole meaning.

  • Check, do not assume: ask the person to tell you what they have understood.
  • Watch for feedback: a frown or silence is information, not rudeness.
  • Close the loop: repeat back key details to confirm you have them right.

Taking a few seconds to confirm understanding prevents most misunderstandings before they cause harm.

params.taskDescription: Drag each word into the gap to complete this description of the communication cycle.
params.textField: The *sender* has a message and chooses how to share it. The *receiver* takes it in and makes sense of it, then gives *feedback* so the sender can *check* that the message was understood.
